dialogues: You've been working with arrays -- describing them by rows or columns, writing expressions for what you see, finding products. Let's see how it's going.
Check out this array. Let's describe what we see by looking at the rows, then build the expression that matches.
That's it. 2 rows of 7. So that's 2 times 7.
This array is 5 rows of 4. But here's the thing -- you can describe the same array by looking at columns instead. Which expression matches if we describe it by columns?
Right. 4 times 5. Four columns of 5. Same array, just described a different way. Different expression.
You can describe this array by rows or by columns. Your pick. Build the equation that matches what you see.
That's it. 6 times 3 equals 18. You counted 6 rows with 3 in each row.
Exactly. 3 times 6 equals 18. You saw 3 columns with 6 in each column.
